items.common_finding_summary: Services lack machine identity authentication suitable for agent principals
items.root_cause_pattern: No OAuth2 client credentials, per-agent API keys, or mTLS configured
items.remediation: Implement OAuth2 client credentials or per-agent API keys with principal attribution

remediation_detail.immediate_action: Deploy shared OAuth2 authorization server (Amazon Cognito) with client credentials grant
remediation_detail.target_state: Token-based M2M authentication with per-agent principal attribution

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No machine identity authentication mechanism suitable for agent principals. SIMPLE auth is trust-based with no credential verification. No OAuth2/OIDC support. No API key management with principal attribution. S3 REST auth is disabled by default.

No machine identity with principal attribution for API-key-authenticated requests. API keys identify a workspace, not an agent principal. Audit logs do not record which API key was used for data operations.
No support for individual machine identity. All API consumers share a single identity. No principal attribution in audit logs.
No machine identity authentication mechanism exists. No OAuth2, API key validation, mTLS, or any form of credential verification. Any client can call all endpoints without proving identity.
No support for per-agent machine identity. A single shared API key cannot provide principal attribution in audit logs.
No machine identity authentication with principal attribution. Cannot distinguish which client made a specific API call.
No machine identity authentication. Cannot distinguish which agent or service made a call.
No dedicated machine identity authentication mechanism exists. An agent cannot authenticate as an independent principal separate from a human user.

No OAuth2 client credentials flow, no API key with principal attribution, no mTLS-based service account mechanism designed for agent identities. Existing auth mechanisms target human users or inter-node communication.
No machine identity authentication exists. Any caller can invoke any API operation without identification.
No machine identity authentication exists. An agent calling this system cannot be identified, attributed, or distinguished from any other caller.
No machine identity authentication on the agent-facing API. IP-based ACL provides no principal attribution.

No machine identity authentication mechanism exists that can distinguish which agent made a call. The system authenticates S3 access keys but has no concept of machine/agent identity attribution beyond the access key itself.
No dedicated machine identity mechanism distinguishable from human users in audit logs. PATs inherit the human user's identity and permissions.
No machine identity authentication. Agents must authenticate using human-style username/password login. No way to distinguish agent principals from human principals.

items.common_finding_summary: No formal, versioned API documentation for agent tool binding
items.root_cause_pattern: APIs defined programmatically without formal specification documentation
items.remediation: Create OpenAPI specifications documenting all routes, schemas, and authentication requirements

remediation_detail.immediate_action: Generate OpenAPI spec from existing route definitions
remediation_detail.target_state: Versioned OpenAPI spec maintained alongside code with breaking-change detection in CI

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No formal, versioned, documented API contract exists. Agents cannot reliably bind to an undocumented interface.
No documented API interface. Integration requires source code reverse-engineering.

items.remediation: Implement attribute-based access control (ABAC) with fine-grained per-agent permission scoping

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No operation-level permission scoping. An agent with read access to a path can invoke any read operation without differentiation.
Non-enterprise API keys lack granular permission scoping. An agent API key cannot be restricted to read-only access on specific chatflows.
No documented guidance or pre-built role for agent identities with least-privilege defaults. An admin creating an agent token must manually determine the minimal permission set.
No mechanism to grant an agent read-only access to specific resources. Every authenticated caller has identical full-admin privileges.
No scoped permission enforcement. An agent identity cannot be granted read-only access to specific resources without inheriting broader privileges.
No scoped permission model exists. Any caller (including a potential agent) would have identical unrestricted access to all endpoints.
No least-privilege enforcement. An agent with the API key can perform any operation including destructive ones.
No least-privilege enforcement. An agent intended for read-only queries has the same access as a system administrator.
No scoped permissions. An agent with the API key can read, write, and delete any resource.
No resource-instance-level permission scoping. An agent identity with 'view apps' permission sees all apps in the organization.
No fine-grained endpoint-level permission scoping. An agent granted READ on datasources gets READ on all matching datasources.
No permission scoping exists. An agent identity cannot be restricted to read-only access or limited to specific resources.
No scoped permission model exists. Any consumer with the DSN can send any event type to the Sentry project.
No infrastructure-as-code defining IAM roles, API Gateway policies, or platform-level permission scoping for agent identities.
Without scoped permissions, an agent identity cannot be restricted to read-only access on specific resources.
All callers from allowed IPs have identical access to all API operations.
IAM-level scoped permissions may be incomplete. Service user bypass means any identity designated as a service user gets full access.
No fine-grained permission scoping below the three fixed authority levels. An agent operating as TENANT_ADMIN inherits full CRUD on all tenant resources.
No fine-grained per-resource permission scoping. Agent identities inherit full role permissions without restriction.

items.remediation: Define agent-aware DR procedures with point-in-time recovery and agent action replay

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No per-identity or per-client request rate limiting. Rate limits are bandwidth-based or server-health-based, all disabled by default.
Rate limiting covers prediction endpoints only. Administrative APIs are unprotected against traffic storms.
A runaway agent loop could make thousands of API calls per second with no throttling. No defense against agent-induced traffic storms.
A runaway agent loop can overwhelm the application at machine speed with no protection.
Rate limiting not active by default. Write endpoints have no rate limiting. Runaway agent loops could overwhelm the server.
No rate limiting exists. A runaway agent could overwhelm the service by calling POST endpoints repeatedly, causing CPU exhaustion and denial of service.
A runaway agent loop could overwhelm the application at machine speed. No protection against brute-force or denial-of-service.
No protection against agent traffic storms overwhelming the application.
No inbound API-layer rate limiting.
No global API rate limiting. An agent calling endpoints at machine speed would face no throttling.
Unlimited API requests possible from any client. No request-level rate limiting.
No API-level rate limiting to prevent runaway agent loops from overwhelming the application.
No configurable client-side rate limit to prevent agent loops from overwhelming the outbound queue.
No per-client or per-endpoint rate limiting to prevent runaway agent loops.
Query endpoints are unprotected from agent traffic storms. A runaway agent loop would not be throttled.
Management API endpoints are unprotected against traffic storms from runaway agents.
No per-agent rate limiting. Disabled by default. Fails open. Service users exempt.
SYS_ADMIN exemption from rate limiting creates risk if agent operates at admin level. No per-identity rate limiting — runaway agent shares tenant pool.
No rate limiting at any layer. Agent or attacker traffic could overwhelm the application.

items.remediation: Implement short-lived OAuth2 tokens with centralized revocation capability

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No API or mechanism to disable a specific agent identity in real-time. No revocation list or token blacklist.
No suspend/disable mechanism for API keys (only permanent deletion). No automated anomaly detection to trigger suspension.
No single kill switch API to suspend a specific agent identity by token identifier. Suspension requires multi-step process with potential delay.
Cannot isolate a misbehaving agent without disrupting all API consumers.
Cannot isolate a misbehaving agent without taking down the broader platform or applying network-level blocks.
All-or-nothing key revocation. Cannot isolate a misbehaving agent without disrupting all integrations.
Cannot isolate a misbehaving agent without taking down all API consumers.
Cannot isolate misbehaving agent without disrupting all consumers.
No independent agent identity suspension mechanism. Suspending an agent would require disabling the human user account.
No immediate, propagated suspension mechanism. Cache-based auth means a non-zero window between disabling and actual lockout.
No mechanism to suspend or revoke individual agent identities.
No programmatic API for identity suspension within the library.
No mechanism to immediately suspend or revoke a specific agent identity without impacting other consumers.
Cannot isolate a misbehaving agent without network-level blocking.
Cannot suspend individual agent access without affecting all callers.
No immediate identity suspension mechanism within the application. Requires external Vault operations or server restart.
No independent, rapid agent identity suspension mechanism. Revoking agent access requires disabling the underlying user account.
Cannot immediately suspend an agent identity and invalidate its active sessions.

items.remediation: Implement automated credential rotation via AWS Secrets Manager (≤ 90 day rotation)

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No action-level authorization. Cannot restrict an agent to read-only within the gRPC or REST API independently of file-system permissions.
No mechanism to restrict an admin-created agent token to read-only operations if the creating user is an admin. The admin bypass is a blast-radius risk.
Cannot enforce that an agent may read albums but not delete them within the same resource type.
Cannot enforce action-level restrictions — an agent granted any access could register, cancel, or modify instances.
No action-level authorization exists. An agent intended for read-only access could invoke code-generation (POST) endpoints without restriction.
Cannot restrict an agent to read-only operations at the application level.
Cannot restrict an agent to read operations while denying delete within the same resource type.
Cannot restrict an agent to read records but not delete them within the same resource type.
Action-level authorization exists but cannot be independently configured per machine identity.
Cannot enforce action-level granularity (read vs write vs delete).
No ABAC or fine-grained RBAC exists within the library.
Cannot enforce action-level policies such as 'agent can read traces but cannot submit spans' at the application layer.
A read-only agent could invoke POST endpoints (pause/resume, retryFailedCRR, applyWorkflowConfiguration).
Action-level authorization available via bucket policies but IAM-level action enforcement may be incomplete per noted TODO.
Action-level authorization is not configurable per identity. A TENANT_ADMIN can always read AND delete within the same resource type.
Cannot grant read-without-write access at the user role level for owned resources.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in agent action audit trail
items.remediation: Instrument structured audit logging with agent identity, action type, and target resource

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: Audit logging disabled by default. When enabled, logs are local files with no tamper-evident or centralized log storage.
No audit trail for data operations (only login events). No immutable storage. No principal attribution for API-key-authenticated requests in audit logs.
No immutable, tamper-evident audit trail. No per-caller attribution in logs or history.
No immutable, tamper-evident audit log for API operations. No centralized audit logging. API operation logs do not record which principal performed the action.
Cannot attribute API actions to specific callers. Log files are not immutable or tamper-evident.
No authenticated principal attribution in logs. No immutable log storage.
Audit logs stored in mutable application database with no tamper-evidence or immutability guarantees. No external immutable log sink configured.
Audit logs are not tamper-evident. Database administrators with metadata store access can modify or delete entries without detection. No CloudTrail integration.
No immutable audit logging for any API operation.
Audit events generated but stored in mutable datastore. No immutable, tamper-evident audit log storage.
No audit trail identifies which caller performed which operation.
No audit trail attributing API actions to specific callers.
Audit logging exists but is not immutable — file-based logs can be modified or deleted. Logging disabled by default.
Audit logs are mutable — can be modified or deleted by database admin. No tamper-evident mechanism.
No audit trail for agent or human actions. Cannot determine who performed which operation.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in data retention / expiry
items.remediation: Define and enforce data retention policies with automated expiry

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: Log sanitization is opt-in, not default. Chat message content (potentially containing PII) is logged and stored without redaction. No automated PII detection.
No mechanism to prevent PII from appearing in application logs. Agent-initiated requests that trigger error paths may log request details including user-related fields.
No log scrubbing for sensitive data. Stack traces in error responses.
Reactive pattern-based redaction rather than proactive field-allowlisting. New credential formats may not be covered.
Trace-level body logging could capture user-submitted data. Regex scrubbing is not guaranteed complete for all patterns.
Log sanitization covers credentials but not broader PII.
No PII redaction in logging pipeline. User-identifying information and potentially sensitive data source credentials may appear in application logs and traces.
No PII redaction in logs. User-submitted query text, identifiers, and configuration payloads logged verbatim.
No PII redaction in logs. Workflow input/output data may leak into log files without filtering.
Healthcare data in request/response bodies can leak into application logs.
No log redaction middleware for PII that may arrive via span annotations.
Object keys and bucket names (which could contain PII) logged without redaction.
No PII redaction in access logs. Object keys, access key IDs, and IP addresses logged in full.
No log scrubbing or PII masking middleware in the logging pipeline.
No PII masking in logs when debug mode is active.

items.remediation: Implement event sourcing or state versioning capturing acting principal per mutation

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No circuit breaker pattern for external UFS calls. Cascading failures from unresponsive storage backends are not isolated.
No circuit breaker pattern. Agent requests could cascade failures through external dependencies.
No circuit breaker pattern for external dependency calls. A failing data source connector could cascade failures back to calling agents.
No circuit breaker (Resilience4j, Hystrix) on external calls. Failing downstream service could cascade through task execution.
No circuit breaker implementation. Cascading failures from external dependencies not prevented.
No circuit breakers, no retry logic with backoff, no configurable timeouts on external dependency calls.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in data classification & scoping
items.remediation: Implement data classification labels and API response scoping per agent authorization level

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: Agent-facing API responses include plaintext secrets. No DTO separation for sensitive vs non-sensitive data.
Agent-facing API responses include sensitive credentials. HostConfig endpoint returns system API key and password hash.
Sensitive credentials returned in general API responses without filtering. No access control differentiation.
Service user bypass provides undifferentiated access to all data. No granularity between sensitive and non-sensitive data access below bucket-policy level.
No field-level sensitivity annotations; UserCredentials.password relies on service-layer discipline; no differentiation of API scopes by data sensitivity.
B2 finding: No access control differentiation. All authenticated users access the same analytics data without scope-based restrictions.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in pii/sensitive data handling
items.remediation: Deploy PII detection/masking middleware for agent-facing API responses

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No data residency configuration, no region enforcement.
No data residency controls. Service instance metadata replicated across peer nodes could cross region boundaries without governance.
No application-level data residency enforcement or documentation.
No data residency enforcement or awareness at the application layer. No region-tagging of data.
No data residency enforcement. Healthcare PHI with no controls preventing agent transmission to LLM endpoints in other jurisdictions.
Data residency configuration exists at storage layer but no controls prevent data egress via API once read.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in service discovery metadata
items.remediation: Implement standardized service discovery metadata (well-known URIs, capability manifests)

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No schema versioning, no breaking change detection in CI pipeline. Agent tool bindings could break silently.
No automated breaking change detection. No consumer-driven contract tests. No formal API changelog.
Agent tool bindings could break silently when the API changes between versions.
No automated breaking change detection. Agents can be broken silently by new releases.
No automated contract validation. Agent tool schemas could break silently when API responses change.
No API versioning, no breaking change detection. Changes to the GenApi interface could break agent tool bindings without notice.
No automated breaking change detection. Agent tool schemas could break silently.
No automated breaking-change detection in CI pipeline.
No automated breaking change detection.
No API versioning strategy. No breaking change detection tooling in CI pipeline.
No automated API contract stability validation in CI.
No API compatibility checking tools (japicmp, revapi) or consumer-driven contract tests in CI pipeline. Public API surface changes could break downstream Flink applications without early detection.
No API versioning strategy. No breaking change detection in pipeline.
No automated API surface change detection in CI pipeline.
No automated contract testing in CI. Breaking API changes not automatically detected.
No breaking change detection mechanism in CI pipeline.
Agent tool bindings could break silently on any deployment.
No formal API contract testing or schema diffing in CI.
No API versioning strategy or breaking change detection.
No automated breaking-change detection in the CI pipeline. API contract stability relies on manual review and deprecation warnings rather than tooling.
Agent tool bindings could break silently on any deployment.

items.remediation: Standardize on structured JSON logging with consistent field schemas

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: Logs not structured (JSON). No trace ID in application logs. Tracing is auto-instrumented but logs are not correlated.
No distributed tracing. No trace/correlation ID in structured logs. Cannot reconstruct agent-initiated request flows.
Traces are optional; logs lack correlation IDs. Agent-initiated requests cannot be easily traced end-to-end.
Cannot trace agent-initiated requests through the system.
Cannot trace agent-initiated requests. Logs are unstructured and lack correlation IDs.
Cannot trace agent-initiated requests through the system. No structured logs for automated analysis.
Cannot trace agent-initiated requests. No structured logging by default. No request correlation.
No distributed tracing. No correlation IDs across request lifecycle.
No distributed tracing or globally unique correlation IDs.
Distributed tracing is available only in Enterprise/Cloud editions. CE deployment has no trace ID propagation.
No full distributed tracing. No structured JSON logging by default.

No trace context propagation or structured logging support for consuming applications. Consuming applications cannot correlate connector-internal log events with their own trace context without additional instrumentation work.
No distributed tracing. No structured JSON logging. Cannot trace agent-initiated requests.
SDK internal diagnostics lack structured logging, but this is expected for a library.
No trace context propagation. No JSON structured logging. No consistent correlation_id.
Non-JSON logs, no request correlation IDs for inbound API calls.
No trace ID propagation for correlating requests across service boundaries.
No distributed tracing. Cannot trace agent-initiated requests through backend dependencies.
Cannot trace agent-initiated requests across services. No request-level correlation.
Agent-initiated requests cannot be traced through the system.

items.remediation: Instrument OpenTelemetry with trace context propagation

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: No alerting thresholds configured for error rates or latency.
No alerting thresholds. No alert routing. API degradation would go undetected.
Metrics are collected but no alerting thresholds configured within the application.
No proactive alerting when the API degrades.
No alerting configured for API degradation.
Service degradation would not be detected until users report failures.
No automated detection of API degradation from agent traffic.
No automated alerting on API degradation.
No error rate or latency alerting on the API surface.

No pre-defined alerting thresholds for agent-relevant error rates.
No monitoring/alerting guidance in documentation. Consumers must independently determine appropriate error rate and latency thresholds for connector operations.
No alert rules for error rate spikes or latency degradation.
No alerting infrastructure defined.
No alerting thresholds for agent-consumed APIs.
No alerting on the management API server's error rate and latency.
No alerting infrastructure deployment in this repo. No auth failure rate alerts.
No automated alerting. Degradation affecting agent consumers would not trigger proactive notifications.
No operational alerting for API degradation affecting agents.

items.common_finding_summary: Portfolio-wide gap in rate limiting / throttling
items.remediation: Deploy API Gateway with rate limiting and usage plans per agent identity

affected_services.resolution_reasoning: The /api/v1/paths/ REST endpoints lack structured error responses with error codes and retryability indicators.
No machine-readable error codes. No retryable indicator. Agents cannot distinguish retriable vs terminal errors programmatically.
Error responses lack a retryable field or error code that agents can use to distinguish transient from permanent errors.
An agent cannot programmatically distinguish retriable from terminal errors without parsing message text.
Agents cannot distinguish retriable errors from terminal errors. No error code, category, or retryable indicator.
No standardized error response structure with retryable classification to help agents distinguish retriable from terminal failures.
No retryable/terminal classification in error responses. No standardized machine-readable error codes.
No machine-readable error code or retryable indicator.
Agent must infer retryability from HTTP status codes alone.
Error responses lack a retryable field. Agents cannot programmatically determine whether to retry without parsing message strings.
Inconsistent error format across endpoints. No explicit retryability signal.
No machine-readable error code field — relies on HTTP status and message text.
Missing retryable indicator. Agents must infer from HTTP status codes.
Agents cannot programmatically distinguish error types or determine retryability.
Agents cannot programmatically distinguish retriable from terminal errors.
No retryable indicator in error responses.
No retryable field or retry-category in error responses.
Agents cannot programmatically distinguish retriable errors from terminal errors.

recommended_actions.name: AI Driven Development Lifecycle
recommended_actions.trigger_reason: Engineering maturity gaps: ENG-Q1, ENG-Q2, ENG-Q3 each RISK-QUALITY in 13 of 18 applicable repos (72%). Manual development workflows predominate.
recommended_actions.suggested_timing: Run first to establish AI-driven development practices
recommended_actions.what_it_provides: Workshop for adopting AI Driven Development Lifecycle — AI-powered execution with human oversight and dynamic team collaboration.

recommended_actions.name: AgentStorming
recommended_actions.trigger_reason: No defined agent use cases; all services read-only scope. Portfolio context describes validation/testing without production agent integration paths.
recommended_actions.suggested_timing: After AI DLC to identify where agents should operate
recommended_actions.what_it_provides: Structured workshop using Cognitive Complexity Analysis and Agentic Workflow Design to pinpoint where AI agents deliver real value.

recommended_actions.name: Agent Experience Engagement
recommended_actions.trigger_reason: 15 services at Agent-Ready profile (>= 3 threshold). Portfolio positioned for structured agent experience design.
recommended_actions.suggested_timing: After AgentStorming to design agent experience for identified use cases
recommended_actions.what_it_provides: Strategic methodology for implementing agentic AI — process mapping, task identification, metrics, data architecture, governance, guardrails.

recommended_actions.name: EBA on Agentic AI
recommended_actions.trigger_reason: AUTH-Q1 (Machine Identity Authentication) is cross-cutting BLOCKER in 15 repositories (threshold: 5+). Systemic identity gap requires coordinated architecture remediation.
recommended_actions.suggested_timing: After AXE to accelerate implementation of cross-cutting remediation
recommended_actions.what_it_provides: Intensive engagement embedding AWS expertise to compress multi-quarter remediation into focused sprint.

portfolio_level_findings.finding: No shared identity provider detected across any repository. Each service uses independent authentication (custom API keys, basic auth, IP-based ACLs).
portfolio_level_findings.evidence: 15 services have AUTH-Q1 BLOCKER; no shared IdP resource appears in 2+ repos.
portfolio_level_findings.recommendation: Deploy centralized identity provider (Amazon Cognito) for M2M authentication across all services.

portfolio_level_findings.finding: No shared trace ID propagation or centralized audit trail. Services log independently with no correlation mechanism.
portfolio_level_findings.evidence: OBS-Q2 RISK-QUALITY in 13 services; no OpenTelemetry or shared tracing configuration found.
portfolio_level_findings.recommendation: Deploy centralized observability (AWS X-Ray or OpenTelemetry) with trace context propagation.

portfolio_level_findings.finding: No shared API gateway, WAF, or portfolio-level rate limiting. API-Q3 RISK-QUALITY in 13 services.
portfolio_level_findings.evidence: No shared WAF WebACL or API Gateway usage plans configured across the portfolio.
portfolio_level_findings.recommendation: Deploy shared API Gateway with WAF and usage plans for portfolio-level rate limiting.
portfolio_level_findings.affected_services: All 27 services with HTTP API surfaces

portfolio_level_findings.finding: No transitive dependency chains identified. Services are independent open-source mirrors that do not interact.
portfolio_level_findings.evidence: No dependency_overrides provided; no inter-service dependencies inferable.
portfolio_level_findings.recommendation: No action required. Re-assess if services are deployed as integrated system.

portfolio_level_findings.finding: No centralized mechanism to suspend/revoke agent identities across services. Each service manages auth independently.
portfolio_level_findings.evidence: AUTH-Q7 RISK-SAFETY in 14 services; no centralized identity governance detected.
portfolio_level_findings.recommendation: Implement centralized agent identity governance with global suspend/revoke capability.

dependency_map.note: No explicit dependency information provided. Services are independent open-source mirrors that do not interact with each other.
